source_title: #343 The Eternal Pursuit of Unhappiness: David Ogilvy

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: This book is about philosophy, ethos, beliefs. It asks: Can a two billion dollar gorilla like Ogilvy ever be svelte, sultry, and sexy again? The Ogilvy agency of the 1950s and 1960s was far hotter than all their competitors combined. The answer to our dilemma can be summed up in two pithy words: divine discontent. Decades before advertising prophets were preaching 'good enough,' Ogilvy was already practicing it. Here's an interpretation: Don't bow your head. Don't know your place. Defy the gods. Don't sit back. Don't give in. Don't give up. Don't win silver medals. Don't be so easily happy with yourself. Don't go gentle into that good night, and don't ever, ever allow a single scrap of rubbish out of this agency.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: That was an excerpt from The Eternal Pursuit of Unhappiness. The subtitle is Being Very Good is No Good. You have to be very, very, very, very good. It's written by the team at Ogilvy and Mather and David Ogilvy. This unexpected episode came about because I've been studying Quentin Tarantino. After finishing that book, I picked this back up. I didn't think I could do an episode on it—it's only 65 pages. Then I remembered Danny Meyer's idea: whoever wrote the rule. Danny would say, whoever wrote the rule that you can't make a high-quality hot dog cart. That was the first version of Shake Shack, now a $4.5 billion company. I realized I was making up fake rules. Whoever wrote the rule that the book has to be 300 pages for me to do an episode on.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: This book was written to solve a problem: what do you do when it's not a founder-led company anymore? The founder has passed away, but we still want to use his ideas to craft company culture. David Ogilvy had this idea of divine discontent. He said, we have a habit of divine discontent with our performance. It's an antidote to smugness. You find this in a lot of top performers. Steve Jobs has a great quote: what do you do when you do something wonderful? You just wake up the next day and try to do something wonderful again. That is divine discontent.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: They came up with eight habits of highly creative communities. To the ancient Greeks, a good habit was called a virtue, while a bad habit was known as a vice. Each of these eight habits has a corresponding evil twin. There's an old Latin proverb: a nail is driven out by a nail. A habit is overcome by another habit. Companies operate out of fear—we want to operate with courage. Companies optimize for expedience—we want idealism. Companies stick to the status quo—we want to be curious. Most companies are boring—we want to be playful. Most companies have a tyranny of politeness—we want a culture of candor. Most companies are run on cold arithmetic—we will optimize for intuition. Most companies fall into bureaucracy—we want a culture of free spiritedness. Most people give in—we will persist.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Habit number one is courage. There's no shortage of brains in the industry. It's the vertebrae column that tends to be missing. Peter Thiel said, brilliant thinking is rare, but courage is in even shorter supply than genius. Fear is a demon that devours the soul of a company. It diminishes the quality of our imagination. It dulls our appetite for adventure, and it sucks away our youth. Fear leads to self-doubt, which is the worst enemy of creativity. Arnold Schwarzenegger said, when you start doubting yourself, that is very dangerous. If fear is our principal adversary, then courage is our chief ally. It is the habit that guarantees all the others. In the absence of courage, nothing worthwhile can be accomplished.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Only with a spine will we stop double-guessing and express an opinion. Founders know that inherently. A founder's opinion is expressed in the product and company that they built. Apple is Steve Jobs with 10,000 lives. Only by standing up for what we believe in will we begin to build the most precious commodity in the world: trust. Courage leads to trust, which leads to courage, which leads to trust, and upwards and onwards we go. Charlie Munger said trust is one of the greatest economic forces on earth. During Warren and Charlie's first dinner, they realized they shared many ideas. When Charlie returned to Los Angeles, the conversations continued. There was no formal partnership or contractual relationship. The bond was created by a handshake and backed by two Midwesterners who understood and respected the value of one's word.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Habit number two: idealism. We are the people that we've been waiting for.
